## Glossary & Addendum


Modelling vs. Recurring Sources

Framework from @pelletier2024bayesian distinguishes temporal behavior:

Source Type

Definition

Examples

Typical Magnitude

Modelling sources

Model parameters & assumptions

Fallow classification, growth rates

4-262% (highest)

Recurring sources

Measurement errors

Activity data, emission factors

30-50%

Modelling sources vs. recurring sources of uncertainty

"The highest error propagated using Monte Carlo simulations was caused by modelling sources of uncertainty, which calls for special attention to ensure consistency in REDD+ reporting which is essential for securing environmental integrity." [@pelletier2024bayesian]

Strategic implication: Modelling sources can be standardized across jurisdictions (one-time investment), while recurring sources require continuous monitoring (ongoing costs).

Sensitivity Analysis Framework

Sobol indices identify variance contributions:

First-order effects (Si): Direct contribution

Total effects (STi): Direct + interactions

Typical results: Activity data (Si = 0.42) >> Biomass (0.31) >> Combustion factor (0.18) >> Emission factor (0.03)

Complementary methods:

Partial correlation coefficients

Tornado diagrams for parameter ranking

Panama case study [@pelletier2024bayesian]: Fallow classification and carbon accumulation = highest sensitivity

Optimization Strategies

Investment hierarchy (based on Sobol indices + cost):

Intervention

Variance Reduced

Cost

ROI Tier

Priority

Activity data QA/QC

42% → 30% (12 pp)

$30-50k

Very High

1

Enhanced plot sampling

31% → 20% (11 pp)

$50-100k

High

2

Tier 2 allometry

31% → 22% (9 pp)

$15-30k

Very High

3

Ground Cf measurement

18% → 12% (6 pp)

$20-40k

Moderate

4

FTIR emission factors

3% → 2% (1 pp)

$50-100k

Very Low

5

Cost-benefit ranking for uncertainty reduction (Chapter 4 detail)

Temporal aggregation provision:

5-year recalculation: Aggregate uncertainty across crediting period

Recovery mechanism: Over-deducted credits issued if cumulative uncertainty decreases

Typical benefit: 5-10 percentage point reduction from temporal averaging

Example: Individual-year deductions = 492,500 t CO2; Aggregated = 406,700 t CO2 → Recovery: 85,800 t CO2

Software Systems

All analyses in this book use R (≥4.2.0) with the following packages:

Core packages:

# Statistical analysis
library(MASS)      # Multivariate normal sampling
library(boot)      # Bootstrap resampling
library(caret)     # Cross-validation

# Monte Carlo simulation
library(mc2d)      # Two-dimensional Monte Carlo
library(sensitivity) # Sobol sensitivity analysis

# Visualization
library(ggplot2)   # Publication-quality graphics
library(plotly)    # Interactive plots

Specialized packages:

# Allometry
library(BIOMASS)   # Pantropical allometric equations
library(allodb)    # Global allometry database

# Spatial analysis
library(terra)     # Raster processing
library(sf)        # Vector spatial data
library(exactextractr) # Zonal statistics

# Reporting
library(knitr)     # Dynamic documents
library(rmarkdown) # Report generation

Key Terminology

Precision vs. Accuracy:

Precision: Reproducibility of measurements (random error)

Accuracy: Closeness to true value (systematic bias)

Confidence intervals:

90% CI: Range containing true value with 90% probability

Half-width: Distance from mean to CI bound = (Upper - Lower) / 2

Error types:

Type 1: False rejection of null hypothesis (α error)

Type 2: False acceptance of null hypothesis (β error)

Type 3: Solving the wrong problem (asking the wrong question)

Type 4: Applying results outside valid domain (extrapolation)

IPCC Tiers:

Tier 1: IPCC default methods and parameters

Tier 2: Country-specific methods or parameters

Tier 3: Higher-order methods (models, repeated measurements)

LULC Approaches:

Approach 1: Total area change (no spatial data)

Approach 2: Tracking of land-use conversions (transition matrices)

Approach 3: Spatially explicit tracking (wall-to-wall maps)
